coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient, ultralong-lifetime, phosphorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots via multi-confinement for anticounterfeiting applications

coredata.dc:description: The highly efficient emission of carbon dots continues to attract significant interest due to their vast potential in optoelectronic and anticounterfeiting applications. Despite their exceptional fluorescence, their room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) remains deficient. Herein, nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) were designed using a simple molecular engineering approach, achieving an impressive phosphorescence quantum yield (PQY) of 48.50 % and a prolonged lifetime of 2.06 s, visible to the naked eye for approximately 22 s. The proposed methodology enhances the formation of high-density (n, π∗) configurations within the CDs by incorporating numerous functional groups such as –NH2 and CO/CN. These functional groups induce robust spin-orbit coupling, thereby facilitating efficient intersystem crossing from singlet to triplet states and promoting radiative recombination from triplet states to ground states. Utilizing NCDs as triplet donors, yellow and red phosphorescent composites were successfully fabricated through effective phosphorescence Förster resonance energy transfer. These multicolor phosphorescent CDs enable advanced applications in time-dependent information storage and encryption. Their dual fluorescent and phosphorescent properties facilitate multilevel authentication through distinct color changes under varying lighting conditions, enhancing security features and rendering these composites highly effective for sophisticated anticounterfeiting solutions.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient, ultralong-lifetime, phosphorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots via multi-confinement for anticounterfeiting applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The highly efficient emission of carbon dots continues to attract significant interest due to their vast potential in optoelectronic and anticounterfeiting applications. Despite their exceptional fluorescence, their room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) remains deficient. Herein, nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) were designed using a simple molecular engineering approach, achieving an impressive phosphorescence quantum yield (PQY) of 48.50 % and a prolonged lifetime of 2.06 s, visible to the naked eye for approximately 22 s. The proposed methodology enhances the formation of high-density (n, π∗) configurations within the CDs by incorporating numerous functional groups such as –NH2 and CO/CN. These functional groups induce robust spin-orbit coupling, thereby facilitating efficient intersystem crossing from singlet to triplet states and promoting radiative recombination from triplet states to ground states. Utilizing NCDs as triplet donors, yellow and red phosphorescent composites were successfully fabricated through effective phosphorescence Förster resonance energy transfer. These multicolor phosphorescent CDs enable advanced applications in time-dependent information storage and encryption. Their dual fluorescent and phosphorescent properties facilitate multilevel authentication through distinct color changes under varying lighting conditions, enhancing security features and rendering these composites highly effective for sophisticated anticounterfeiting solutions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials with long emission lifetimes have received considerable attention owing to their potential applications such as high-sensitivity bioimaging, information anti-counterfeiting, decoration, and optoelectronic devices [1–3]. Early RTP materials focused on inorganic substances, which faced significant drawbacks such as expensive and complex synthesis, heavy metal toxicity, and poor processability [4,5]. To overcome these issues, metal-free pure organic RTP materials were rapidly developed. However, these materials suffer from weak spin-orbit coupling (SOC) and high non-radiative decay rates, causing triplet excitons to deactivate easily and limiting their lifetimes to only several milliseconds or microseconds [6]. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have gained substantial attention as RTP material, due to excellent photostability, eco-friendly synthesis, and low toxicity, positioning them as a potential candidate for optical communications, light illumination, anticounterfeiting, and information-based encryption [7–9]. Specifically, blue CDs with efficient output have gained considerable attention in RTP [10]. For instance, ionic liquids have proven effective in synthesizing blue-emissive CDs, achieving an impressive photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 98.5 %, the highest reported to date [11]. However, the phosphorescence quantum yield (PQY) remains suboptimal and underdeveloped compared to PLQY. This limitation is primarily due to the inefficiency of spin-flip transitions, which hinders the full potential of these materials for practical applications [12,13].

For effective RTP emission, two essential criteria must be fulfilled. Firstly, there should be a proficient intersystem crossing (ISC) from the first excited singlet level (S1) to a higher-energy triplet level (Tn, with n ≥ 1) to generate an adequate population of triplet excitons. Secondly, it is crucial to stabilize these triplet excitons by minimizing thermal dissipation via non-radiative decay and energy-quenching processes from the triplet state (T1) [14,15]. Achieving RTP emission in CDs can be successfully done by integrating them into different matrix materials, such as melamine [16], cyanuric acid [17], biuret [18], silica shells[19], and boric acid [20]. For instance, H. Qiu et al. embedded CDs in a rigid polycrystalline B2O3 matrix, where the three-dimensional spatial encapsulation stabilized the higher-energy triplet state, resulting in blue phosphorescent CDs with a PQY of 4 % [13]. Sun Y. et al. achieved room-temperature phosphorescence in a CD-based material by utilizing a multi-confinement effect from Si to O network, characterized by a highly rigid structure, stable covalent/hydrogen bonds, and sufficient 3D nano-space with a PQY of 26 % [21]. Despite these promising achievements, there have been limited breakthroughs in developing simple methods to achieve blue RTP with high PQY. Additionally, most researchers focus on reducing the band gap or stabilizing triplet states, and few studies have explored enhancing SOC to facilitate ISC, which is crucial for the formation of triplet excitons [20,21]. According to El-Sayed's rule, the formation and radiative decay of the triplet excited state is significantly influenced by the efficiency of the ISC process, which is predominantly controlled by SOC. SOC facilitates the spin-flip transition necessary for ISC [22]. This transition is more efficient when SOC is strong, which depends on the electronic configuration of the involved states [23]. ISC between similar electronic configurations, such as from 1(π, π∗) S1 to 3(π, π∗) Tn or from 1(n, π∗) S1 to 3(n, π∗) Tn is inefficient due to weak SOC resulting in less favorable ISC as shown in Fig. 1b [24]. Conversely, when the electronic configuration differs, such as from 1(π, π∗) S1 to 3(n, π∗) Tn or from 1(n, π∗) S1 to 3(π, π∗) Tn, SOC is stronger, making the spin-flip transition more probable. This difference in configurations allows for spin-flip-allowed transitions, thereby enhancing ISC efficiency [25,26]. Moreover, the electronic configuration of the triplet excited state typically consists of two configurations in different proportions, such as α
n
3 (n, π∗) + β
π
3 (π, π∗). This allows the phosphorescence relaxation to be tunable, as the relative amounts of each configuration can be adjusted [27,28]. CDs inherently feature numerous (π, π∗) transitions due to their distinctive sp2 π-conjugated carbon structure, and achieving this requires carefully designing and fine-tuning the triplet excited state by incorporating suitable (n, π∗) configurations to enhance SOC and improve RTP efficiency [29]. This strategy enhances SOC, which is essential for efficient RTP emission in CDs [30].

Herein, highly efficient metal-free nitrogen-doped CDs with blue RTP emission were synthesized using a two-step facile strategy by incorporating additional (n, π*) configurations into their π–π conjugate system. The NCDs exhibit a high PQY of 48.50 % and a long phosphorescent lifetime of 2.06 s, lasting ≈ 22 s to the naked eye. Our experimental results confirm that various functional groups, such as –NH2 and CO/CN groups, are successfully introduced, providing CDs with additional (n, π*) configurations in their structure and enhancing SOC. Leveraging effective phosphorescence FRET from the triplet excitons of NCDs as donors to singlet states of acceptors, yellow and red composites were successfully fabricated. Finally, these phosphorescent composites have been applied for information security, anticounterfeiting, and multicolor LEDs

3-Aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES) and urea (99.5 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), fluorescein sodium salt, rhodamine and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) were sourced from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All reagents were used as received without additional purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were obtained using a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN instrument. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with an ESCALab 220i-XL electron spectrometer (VG Scientific). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Rigaku Ultima IV system utilizing Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired using a VERTEX-80 (Bruker, Germany). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured with a TU-1900 (Metash, China). Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were conducted using an Edinburgh FS5 spectrophotometer. Phosphorescent emission spectra were captured with a 1 ms delay following the cessation of UV light. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield, phosphorescence spectrum, and phosphorescence lifetime were assessed with an Edinburgh FLS1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer under ambient conditions.

A mixture of 20 mmol (4.68 mL) of APTES and 10 mL of water was placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave, heated at 190°C for 9 hours, and subsequently allowed to cool to room temperature. The product was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min, resulting in a UCDs solution. Further, the UCDs solution was subjected to thermal treatment at 150°C for 120 min in an oil bath, yielding UCDs in powder. The UCDs solution was used as the template for the synthesis of NCDs, GCDs, and YCDs.

NCDs were prepared by mixing 0.3 g of urea, 2 mL of UCDs solution, and 5 mL of deionized water in a 20 mL glass container. The mixture was subjected to thermal treatment at 150°C for 120 min in an oil bath, and subsequently allowed to cool to room temperature, resulting in the formation of NCDs powder.

GCDs were synthesized by mixing 0.3 g of urea, 2 mL of UCDs solution, 0.025 g of NaOH, and 5 mL of deionized water in a 20 mL glass container. The mixture was subjected to thermal treatment at 150°C for 120 min in an oil bath, then subsequently allowed to cool to room temperature, yielding GCDs.

YCDs were synthesized by combining 0.3 g of urea, 2 mL of UCDs solution, 0.8 mg of fluorescein, and 5 mL of deionized water in a 20 mL glass container. The mixture was subjected to thermal treatment at 150°C for 120 min in an oil bath, resulting in YCDs formation.

RCDs were synthesized by combining 0.3 g of urea, 2 mL of UCDs solution, 1.8 mg of rhodamine, and 5 mL of deionized water in a 20 mL glass container. The mixture was subjected to thermal treatment at 150°C for 120 min in an oil bath, resulting in RCDs formation.

To synthesize NCDs@PVP powder, 0.1 g of PVP and 2 mL of UCDs solution were mixed with 5 mL of deionized water in a 20 mL glass container and subjected to thermal treatment at 150°C for 120 min in an oil bath. After cooling to room temperature, the NCDs@PVP powder was obtained.

For the NCDs prepared at low temperatures at 90 °C (NCDs90) and NCDs prepared at high temperature at 190 °C (NCDs190), 0.3 g of urea and 2 mL of UCDs solution were combined with 5 mL of deionized water and subjected to thermal treatment either at 150°C for 120 min or 190°C for 120 min. After cooling naturally, NCDs90 and NCDs190 powders were formed. p-UCDs were formed using physical blends of UCDs and Urea.

The embedding of CDs in a solid rigid matrix helps to enhance the stability of triplet excited states, which are typically less stable than their singlet excited states. However, the transitions in CDs are predominantly of the (π, π*) type and the lack of (n, π*) transitions in CDs severely limits the efficiency of ISC, which restricts the possibility of phosphorescent emission [20]. For efficient phosphorescent emission, transitions involving (n, π*) configurations are desirable, as they can facilitate ISC through SOC, which is a primary mechanism that allows electrons to transition between singlet and triplet states [30]. To enhance the phosphorescent emission of CDs, it is crucial to introduce a significant number of (n, π*) transitions. This can be achieved through molecular engineering by attaching groups with abundant lone-pair electrons to the surface of the CDs. Previous studies have demonstrated that urea is an effective nitrogen dopant and surface modifier because it introduces functional groups that contain lone-pair electrons, such as amino, carbonyl, and imine groups [31]. These groups can modulate the excitation energies and transition orbital characters of the CDs, thus promoting more efficient ISC through SOC. This, in turn, increases the probability of triplet state formation and enhances phosphorescent emission. Therefore, modifying CDs with surface functional groups rich in (n, π*) transitions using a urea treatment strategy is a feasible approach. This modification increases the proportion of α
n
3(n, π*) and β
π
3 (π, π*) transitions, which promotes the ISC process [30]. As a result, this modification can lead to a slower non-radiative decay rate and a faster ISC rate, ultimately improving the phosphorescent emission of CDs. The improved balance between these rates ensures that the triplet states are better populated and maintained, resulting in a more sustained and efficient phosphorescent emission.

Based on the described design concept, urea was employed as a nitrogen dopant to achieve effective blue phosphorescence using an oil bath method. The first step involves the preparation of undoped CDs (UCDs) using 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES) through a hydrothermal approach (Fig. 1a). The UCDs exhibit intense blue luminescence, attributed to the spatial restriction imposed by the silica framework [32]. In the second step, the surface of UCDs was modified using urea in an oil bath to introduce the functional groups that promote the (n, π*) transitions, improving phosphorescence emission [33].

As shown in Fig. 2b, the UCDs exhibit PL emission at 468 nm but do not display (RTP). In contrast, the NCDs show bright blue RTP once the 365 nm irradiation is removed, as depicted in Fig. 2a. This results in a prolonged luminescence lasting up to 22 s, demonstrating a significant phosphorescence emission after the cessation of 365 nm UV light, as can be seen in Video S1 (Supporting Information). As depicted in Fig. 2c, the NCDs display a PL emission peak at 444 nm and an RTP peak centered at 460 nm upon switching off the excitation light, indicating a slight peak shift. The luminescence characteristics of the NCDs are further analyzed using the Commission International de l’Eclairage (CIE) 1931 chromaticity coordinates. Fig. 2d shows that the PL and RTP CIE coordinates are calculated to be (0.20, 0.24) and (0.21, 0.26), respectively, confirming the true-blue emission observed visually. Additionally, the RTP lifetime was measured and fitted to an exponential function. The standard formula for phosphorescence lifetime measurement, as given in Eq. 1, was used to quantify this.

The average lifetime was determined to be 2.06 s, indicating a significant phosphorescent emission as illustrated in Fig. 2e [34]. The UV absorption spectrum of NCDs revealed an absorption peak at 262 nm and additional peaks at 310, 360, and 386 nm (Fig. 2f). These peaks are attributed to the π–π* transition of CC bonds and the n–π* transition of CO/CN bonds [7,35]. Furthermore, the RTP excitation spectrum exhibits a prominent band from 320 nm to 384 nm, which significantly overlaps with the absorption band of the n–π* transition of CO/CN bonds. This overlap suggests that these bonds are effectively contributing to the observed phosphorescence [36,37].

Meanwhile, the PLQY was calculated using an integrating sphere and found to be 68.70 % (Fig. S2). The PQY was determined using the following equation, following previously reported methods [13,32]:

where A and B represent the integrated areas of the total photoluminescence and phosphorescence spectra, respectively. The phosphorescence was separated from PL in a time-gated scheme Fig. S3. The absolute PQY reached 48.50 %. This study demonstrates a substantial improvement in PQY compared to previously reported CDs in the blue waveband. To further optimize the afterglow efficiency different mass amounts of urea (100, 200, 300 and 400 mg) composites were prepared under the same condition as for NCDs with variations of urea content, which were labelled as NCDs100, NCDs200, NCDs300, NCDs400 respectively (Fig. S4). As shown in Fig. S5 except for the fact the UCDs exhibited zero RTP, and all the samples with varying urea concentration resulted in phosphorescent durations ranging from 5 to 22 s to the naked eye, while maintaining the same phosphorescence emission centre. The phosphorescent intensity is found to be seven times higher than those treated with 100 mg urea (Fig. 2g). Subsequently, the absolute PL quantum yield and their corresponding phosphorescence quantum efficiency were measured, and the NCDs300 shows superior performance with the longest lifetime and highest phosphorescent quantum yield. With the oil-bath treatment, CDs can be modified with plenty of surface functional groups, such as CO, CN, or -NH2 groups. These groups could effectively form hydrogen bonds and covalent bonds with the SiO2 to stabilize the singlet/triplet excitons as well as reduce their vibration-induced non-radiative transition [38,39]. Thus, the radiative recombination of CDs can be largely boosted to bring about an increased PL QY of CDs from 12.68 % to 68.5 %. However, both the PL and phosphorescence QYs of NCDs400 experience a slight decrease, which can be attributed to the excess urea may bring in partial non-radiative traps to quench the PL and phosphorescence (Fig. S4 inset).

To investigate the phosphorescence generation, detailed studies were conducted on both UCDs and NCDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, shown in Fig. 3(a-d), reveal that both UCDs and NCDs are predominantly quasi-spherical in shape with a monodispersed distribution, confirming the successful formation of CDs. After nitrogen doping using urea in the oil bath, the average size of the NCDs increased significantly from 4.46 nm for UCDs to 5.43 nm for NCDs, as shown in Fig. 3(h). This size increase is likely due to the attachment of additional functional groups, such as carbonyl (C = O), amino (-NH2), and imine (CN) groups, which resulted from the reactions involving urea during the thermal treatment process [36]. Furthermore, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) measurements shown in Fig. 3(e) and (f), exhibit that both the UCDs and NCDs have crystalline structures with a clear lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) facet of graphite [40]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the NCDs, as shown in Fig. 3(g), results from the superposition of diffraction from multiple crystallites, displaying a combination of bright spots that reflect contributions from various crystallographic planes in all orientations, characteristic of a polycrystalline nature [41]. Moreover, High-Angle Annular Dark Field (HAADF) imaging and Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS) mapping (Fig. 3i) confirmed the presence and distribution of key elements, including carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), and silicon (Si), across the material. These results further validate the successful fabrication of the NCDs. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns in Fig. 3(j-l), further supported this fact, indicating the presence of a non-uniform network of Si-O tetrahedral units as a result of the widening of the SiO2 peak at around 21.90° for UCDs and 21.52° for NCDs confirming that CDs successfully integrated within the SiO2 matrix [21]. Additionally, a comparison of the XRD patterns of urea, UCDs, and NCDs reveals no characteristic peaks associated with urea. This, along with the observation that the size of NCDs is larger than that of UCDs, suggests that some of the urea precursors have reacted with UCDs during the oil-bath urea treatment process. This reaction likely led to the formation and attachment of additional functional groups, which was further confirmed using FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 4a, FT-IR results confirm the existence of abundant functional moieties on the surface of both UCDs and NCDs, including amine (-NH2), carboxyl (-COOH), methylene (-CH2), and hydroxyl (-OH) groups [42,43]. Meanwhile, the presence of stretching vibrations at 1133 and 1019 cm−1 corresponds to Si-O-Si and Si-O-C, which confirms the development of covalent bonds linking CDs and silica networks [44,45]. Moreover, the FT-IR spectrum of NCDs shows a notably enhanced absorption band in the range of 3072–3662 cm−1 compared to that of UCDs, attributed to the stretching vibrations from N-H and O-H groups [46].

Furthermore, NCDs exhibit a new peak at 1481 cm−1 attributed to N-H vibrations indicating the presence of numerous amino groups in the NCDs [47]. These amino groups can effectively form hydrogen bonds with the SiO2 matrix, which is anticipated to mitigate non-radiative transitions caused by vibrations. Furthermore, the intensity of CO stretching vibration at 1600 cm−1 significantly rises from UCDs to NCDs. A secondary vibration peak at 1640 cm−1 attributed to the CN vibrational stretching is also observed in NCDs indicating that a greater number of CO and CN bonds have developed on the surface of NCD, suggesting enhanced chemical functionality [48,49]. Additionally, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) shows a higher mass loss for the NCDs compared to the UCDs, which further supports the presence of more surface functional groups, particularly amino (–NH2) and carbonyl (–CO) groups (Fig. S6). These functional groups are more thermally unstable and more prone to degradation, leading to increased mass loss during heating. This observation is consistent with the higher chemical functionality of the NCDs, validating the incorporation of urea-derived groups into their surface. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of both UCDs and NCDs were obtained. The XPS results, illustrated in Fig. 4b, reveal four distinct peaks at approximately 104 eV, 284 eV, 399 eV, and 531 eV, associated with Si 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. This confirms the presence of silicon, carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen in both samples [50,51]. The XPS analysis indicates an increase in the nitrogen and oxygen composition on the surface of NCDs compared to UCDs, suggesting the formation of enhanced N/O-related functional groups [37]. Additionally, Table S1 in the supporting information provides a summary of the high-resolution XPS fitting results for C 1 s and O 1 s peaks, offering a more quantitative view of the changes in functional groups from UCDs and NCDs. This detailed compositional analysis underscores the successful incorporation of nitrogen and oxygen functionalities in NCDs, aligning with the previously discussed FT-IR results. Furthermore, high-resolution XPS was used to analyze, as shown in Fig. 4c, the C 1 s spectra of UCDs exhibit four major peaks at 284.3, 284.6, 285.4, and 288.2 eV which can be attributed to C-Si, CC, C-N, and CO bonds respectively [50,52]. When comparing these spectra to those of NCDs, there is an increase in the CO and C-N bonds, respectively as shown in Table S1. Additionally, a new peak appeared in the NCDs at 289.0 eV, which can be attributed to CN bonds. As shown in Fig. 4d, the O 1 s spectra exhibit peaks at 532.7 eV, 532.2 eV, and 531.7 eV, corresponding to Si-O, C-OH, and CO bonds, respectively. The fitting results for O 1 s indicate an increase in the Si-O and C-OH bonds from UCDs to NCDs, suggesting an enhanced presence of oxygen-containing functional groups (Table S2). Moreover, the N 1 s spectra represent pyridinic N, amino N, and pyrrolic N, showing peaks at 398.9 eV, 399.4 eV, and 400.2 eV, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 4e. The ratio of amino N and pyrrolic N is higher in NCDs compared to UCDs, indicating a greater incorporation of these nitrogen species. Finally, the Si 2p spectrum was deconvoluted into two peaks at 102.8 eV and 102.1 eV, corresponding to C-Si and O-Si bonds, respectively as shown in Fig. 4f [50,52]. The O-Si peak showed a notable enhancement in the NCDs compared to the UCDs. Furthermore, the 13C and 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra ((CD3)2SO, ppm) for NCDs, UCDs, and pure urea precursors were analyzed. As shown in Fig. S7 (a), the 13C NMR spectrum of urea, a peak at 160.6 ppm was observed, corresponding to the urea group [53]. After reacting with the UCDs, the urea group signals from the pure urea precursor disappeared in the NCDs, indicating that the urea groups have been incorporated into the surface of the NCDs. This suggests that urea serves as a source of nitrogen and oxygen, enriching the surface chemistry of the NCDs. Meanwhile, new peaks at 160.2, 156.3, and 150.4 ppm appeared in both UCDs and NCDs, which can be attributed to the -OH, CN, and CO groups, respectively [54–56]. However, the relative signal intensity of CN and CO to -OH peaks was stronger in NCDs compared to UCDs suggesting that the urea-derived groups (CO and -NH2) can be evolved into CO and CN and then anchored on the surface of NCDs during thermal treatment in oil bath. Additionally, the 1H NMR spectra showed the -NH2 signal at 5.4 ppm for NCDs, which can be ascribed to the active hydrogen signal of -NH2 (Fig. S7 b) [57]. The -NH2 signal in the NCDs is significantly enhanced compared to that in the UCDs, further confirming that the urea molecules have effectively reacted with the UCDs. These results confirm that urea treatment introduces -NH2, CO, and CN groups to the surface of the NCDs, which play a crucial role in enhancing the RTP emission. Furthermore, the treatment of NCDs with NaOH induces ionization of carboxyl groups, forming multiple ionic bonds. As shown in Fig. S8, The FT-IR spectra of GCDs show absorption bands at 2250 cm−1 (CN stretch) and 1465 cm−1 (asymmetric stretching vibration of carboxylate groups), indicating the presence of potassium cyanate and ionic bonds [58]. These interactions construct a rigid network, suppressing non-radiative transitions and facilitating RTP-assisted energy transfer as shown in Fig. S9 (a)[59]. The confinement of CDs in this matrix leads to a redshift in delayed emission from 407 nm to 514 nm (Fig. S9). The calculated ΔEST (0.49 eV) supports an efficient ISC process, promoting triplet-exciton formation. Hydrogen bonds between -OH and -NH₂ groups further reduce non-radiative transitions, as supported by thermogravimetric analysis [60]. The structural rigidity enhances phosphorescence, and GCDs exhibit afterglow emission in ethanol solvents, lasting up to 5 s. This observation highlights the role of ionization and rigidity in accelerating ISC and boosting triplet-exciton formation, making the system efficient in emitting phosphorescence in both solid and liquid states.

Multicolor phosphorescent emission from a single fluorescent guest using FRET, which mediates the energy of T1 exciton from the donor towards the S1 of the acceptor, has been demonstrated as an efficient and robust approach (Fig. 7b). Based on this principle, it is possible to regulate the phosphorescent emission color of CDs by selecting fluorescent entities with varying S1 energy levels [61]. To illustrate this concept, we have synthesized two composites using fluorescein and rhodamine. These were used to prepare the composite materials, yellow emissive phosphorescent (YCDs), and red emissive phosphorescent (RCDs) showcasing the ability to achieve varied phosphorescent colors by appropriately selecting different fluorescence guests [62]. A comprehensive description of the synthesis method can be found in the experimental section. The GCDs and YCDs exhibit a phosphorescent lasting up to 7 s, visible to the naked eye without the need for special equipment, as demonstrated in Fig. 5a and videos S2 and S3 in the supporting information. The XRD pattern of GCDs and YCDs indicates the successful encapsulation of both the donor and acceptor within the SiO2 matrix (Fig. 5b). The absorption spectra of YCDs overlap with the RTP spectrum of NCDs, fulfilling the requirement for efficient energy transfer from triplet states to singlets, as shown in Fig. 5c [61]. The RTP spectrum of the GCDs and YCDs is further analyzed using the CIE. As depicted in Fig. 5d, the RTP CIE coordinates are calculated to be (0.22, 0.28) for GCDs and (0.45, 0.51) for YCDs, confirming the true green and yellow emission observed visually. The RTP spectrum of GCDs shows emission peaks centered at 480 nm and 560 nm for YCDs as depicted in Fig. 5e. The phosphorescent lifetime of GCDs and YCDs is as long as 0.40 sec and 0.49 s at room temperature (Fig. 5f). Notably, the lifetimes decreased significantly compared to NCDs as shown in Table S2. The decrease in lifetimes in YCDs can be ascribed to the non-radiative energy transfer from NCDs to composite via FRET (Fig. 7b). Furthermore, the RTP excitation and YCDs detected at 398 nm, showing a minor peak at 390 nm and a more prominent peak at 444 nm, are presented in Fig. 5g. These excitation spectra closely match the absorption peak of NCDs, highlighting the potential for a FRET process within YCDs. Furthermore, additional experiments were conducted using Rhodamine B as the energy acceptor to prepare composite materials, termed RCDs. These RCDs exhibited afterglow emission visible to the naked eye for over 8 s ( Fig. 6a). The emission spectra showed a peak at 621 nm, with excitation bands nearly overlapping the absorption peaks of blue phosphorescent CDs, thus supporting the FRET process [63] (Fig. 6b). The red afterglow had a lifetime of 0.42 s, confirming nonradiative energy transfer from NCDs to RCDs via FRET (Fig. 6d). To validate the FRET mechanism, we synthesized Fluorescein@urea and Rhodamine@urea without UCDs. As shown in Fig. 6(e-g) revealed weak yellow and red afterglow emissions, whereas YCDs and RCDs exhibited 160- and 165-fold higher afterglow intensity, with much longer lifetimes. This substantial enhancement confirms that the strong afterglow emission primarily arises from the energy transfer process facilitated by the NCDs.

Based on our experimental results, the RTP mechanism for these CDs can be proposed as follows. Initially, APTES act as a carbon source for carbonization, facilitating the formation of UCDs. Concurrently, APTES can hydrolyze three kinds of silyl ether groups to form an interconnected SiO2 matrix that encapsulates the CDs and potentially developing into CDs/silica monolith [45]. FT-IR and XPS analyses indicate that UCDs primarily consist of carbon core with fewer O/N-modified surface groups, facilitating a combination of (π, π*) and (n, π*) transitions in the CDs [30]. The absence of RTP in UCDs may be associated with the inefficient transition from the singlet state to the triplet state. Additionally, the UCDs experience a rapid rate of non-radiative transition, where the excited electrons lose their energy before they can transition back to the ground state and emitting phosphorescence. This combination of inefficient ISC and fast non-radiative deactivation significantly hinders the observation of phosphorescence in UCDs at room temperature [20]. To introduce RTP, we modified UCDs with urea, incorporating a variety of functional groups such as CO/CN, -NH2, which are abundant in (n, π*) transitions ( Fig. 7a). These groups are beneficial as they strengthen SOC and narrowing the ΔEST, thereby facilitating the ISC from singlet to triplet states [51]. Furthermore, NCDs, enriched with –NH2 and CO/CN surface functional groups, are incorporated within a SiO2 matrix, creating covalent and hydrogen bonds. These interactions effectively limit the intermolecular movement of the CDs, thereby reducing the non-radiative decay of the triplet configurations. Consequently, the phosphorescent emission is significantly improved, promoting the stabilization of triplet states and minimizing energy loss via non-radiative pathways [64]. However, the increased urea concentration in NCDs400 can generate additional non-radiative traps, which act as quenching sites. These sites cause the excited states to lose energy through non-radiative transitions, leading to a reduction in lifetime from 22 s to 10 s, as shown in Fig. S4. Moreover, the composite prepared using embedding UCDs into matrices like polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) did not produce any detectable phosphorescent emission at room temperature (Fig. S10). This indicates that relying solely on hydrogen bonding fixation does not adequately activate the RTP of UCDs.To further investigate the role of -NH2, CO, and CN groups in determining the phosphorescent properties of NCDs, three additional samples were prepared such as physical blends of UCDs and Urea (p-UCDs), NCDs prepared at low temperatures at 90 °C (NCDs90) and NCDs prepared at high temperature at 190 °C (NCDs190). As shown in Fig. S11, no afterglow emission was detected at room temperature when UCDs were physically blended with urea, suggesting that urea does not directly affect the surface states of the CDs. Similarly, NCDs90 showed no phosphorescence, and its XRD pattern was almost identical to that of UCDs, further supporting the lack of surface modification (Fig. S11). At low temperatures, urea is unable to convert into -NH2 or CO/CN groups, which are necessary for phosphorescence, resulting in no RTP emission [65]. In contrast, NCDs190 displayed bright blue phosphorescence, although the PQY decreased from 48.50 % to 25.31 %. As shown in Fig. S12, FT-IR spectrum of NCDs190 revealed that the absorption peaks associated with CN vibrations at 1662 cm⁻¹ and N-H bending at 1560 cm⁻¹ almost completely disappeared, indicating a reduction in the nitrogen-related functional groups on the surface of the CDs at high temperatures. These results emphasize the importance of -NH2 and CN groups in generating efficient phosphorescence in the CDs.Therefore, the presence of lone-pair-electron-containing groups such as carbonyl/imine/amino is essential for generating effective phosphorescence in CDs. These groups introduce numerous (n, π*) transitions, which are crucial for phosphorescence.

Counterfeiting and inverse engineering of security confidential has significantly increased, which is a major challenge for governments, companies, and customers. Recent advancements in anticounterfeiting and authentication technologies focus on materials like fluorescent, phosphorescent, photochromic, hydrochromic, and thermochromic compounds, which emit luminescence or change color when exposed to external stimuli, such as light, temperature, or moisture [66]. Among these, photochromic materials, including spiropyran, diarylethene, and oxazolidine, are highly effective molecular switches for security applications [67]. However, their performance declines due to negative photochromism in polar environments, limiting their use with hydrophobic polymer nanomaterial carriers. In contrast, CDs that exhibit both fluorescence and phosphorescence have emerged as promising alternatives for creating high-security, multilevel authentication systems. These CDs offer fast authentication, are easy to synthesize at low cost and exhibit excellent chemical and colloidal stability. The high quantum efficiency of NCDs and the adjustable phosphorescent properties of composites such as GCDs and YCDs make them ideal for multi-color time-resolved information encryption, pattern anticounterfeiting, and light illumination applications owing to their dual-mode luminescence and responsiveness to multiple stimuli.

As shown in Fig. 8a, a four-binary digit pattern is illuminated using high-intensity LED light. After switching off the light, the pattern emits a light red color. Under 365 nm UV illumination, the pattern shows white light with the same binary pattern. When the UV light is turned off, a new code "876" appears for 4 s, followed by a single binary number "8" after 6 s, demonstrating the potential for time-dependent information encryption with these composites. Additionally, pattern anticounterfeiting was demonstrated through variations in phosphorescent durations of the composites (Fig. 8b). RTP powders of NCDs, GCDs, and YCDs were evenly distributed on a model of flower, leaves, and stem. In daylight, the powders appeared nearly white. When illuminated with a 365 nm UV lamp, the flower, leaves, and stem glowed blue, yellow, and green, respectively. After the UV lamp was turned off, all three models remained visible for a short period. The brightness of the stem and leaves faded completely within seconds, while the blue flower's brightness persisted for over 6 s. As shown in Fig. 8(c), the UCDs, NCDs, GCDs, and YCDs were employed in a Morse code-like system for encoding and decoding messages, leveraging their dual-mode, time-dependent fluorescent and phosphorescent behavior. When both the fluorescence and RTP properties of composite materials matched, a “1” was denoted in Morse code, if they differed, a “0” was marked.

This approach enabled the creation of Morse code sequences such as “100,” “10,” “01,” “110,” “0,” and “010” for the Latin alphabet letters “R,” “E,” “S,” “C,” “U,” and “E,” respectively, spelling out the word “rescue”. Furthermore, leveraging the phosphorescent emission, highly efficient multicolor LEDs were fabricated using NCDs, GCDs, and YCDs. As shown in Fig. 8d, the LEDs emit light across the visible spectrum, with CIE coordinates confirming the color emission. Notably, even after the UV light is turned off, a phosphorescent is visible to the naked eye for more than 6 s for NCDs and 4 s for GCDs and YCDs. This phosphorescent emission is highly advantageous and can be utilized for producing alternating-current LEDs [68].

In summary, we have developed a method utilizing an oil bath technique to create nitrogen-doped carbon dots with a highly efficient PQY of 48.50 % with a lifetime of 2.06 s. Our findings demonstrate that surface functionalization plays a crucial role in enhancing the PQY of NCDs; specifically, the introduction of abundant –NH₂ and CO/CN groups creates new (n, π*) states that boost SOC and narrow the energy gap between singlet and triplet states, thereby facilitating ISC. Moreover, the presence of covalent and hydrogen bonds between the NCDs and the SiO2 matrix mitigates non-radiative deactivation of the triplet configurations, resulting in longer lifetimes and superior PQY. This research could pave the way for the development of high-efficiency phosphorescent CDs materials with applications in information encryption, lighting, and advanced display technologies. Moving forward, this research establishes a foundation for developing high-efficiency phosphorescent CDs materials, which leverage both fluorescence and phosphorescence as promising alternatives for creating high-security, multilevel authentication systems. Future efforts will concentrate on optimizing these materials for specific applications and investigating their performance under various environmental conditions.
